subject: Chemistry
q: Hemoglobin contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>34</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of iron by mass. The number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Fe</mi></math> atoms in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hemoglobin is (Given : Atomic mass of Fe is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>56</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mo>,</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub></math> in<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>022</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: Hemoglobin contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>34</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of iron by mass. The number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Fe</mi></math> atoms in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hemoglobin is (Given : Atomic mass of Fe is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>56</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mo>,</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub></math> in<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>022</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Mass of haemoglobin = 3.3g

Mass of iron atoms = 0.34100×3.3=0.01122g

56 g of iron contains = 1 mole of iron or 6.023 × 1023 atoms

Number of moles of atoms=Mass atomic mass

Number of moles of 0.01122g of iron is

Number of moles = 0.01122g56g=0.00020 moles

Number of atoms =Number of moles ×avogadro number

No. of Fe atoms

 =0.00020×6.022×1023=1.2046 × 1020

subject: Chemistry
q:
The elemental composition of a compound is $54.2 \% \mathrm{C}, 9.2 \% \mathrm{H}$ and $36.6 \% \mathrm{O}$.
If the molar mass of the compound is $132 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, the molecular formula of the compound is :
[Given : The relative atomic mass of $\mathrm{C}: \mathrm{H}: \mathrm{O}=12: 1: 16$ ]
question:
The elemental composition of a compound is $54.2 \% \mathrm{C}, 9.2 \% \mathrm{H}$ and $36.6 \% \mathrm{O}$.
If the molar mass of the compound is $132 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, the molecular formula of the compound is :
[Given : The relative atomic mass of $\mathrm{C}: \mathrm{H}: \mathrm{O}=12: 1: 16$ ]
solution: Molecular mass of compound $=132 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ Molecular formula of compound is $\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_4 \mathrm{O}\right)_n$ $\mathrm{n}=\frac{\text { Molecular mass }}{\mathrm{EF} \text { mass }}=\frac{132}{44}=3$ $\therefore$ Molecular formula of compound is $\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_3$

subject: Chemistry
q: On complete combustion, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>492</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an organic compound gave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>792</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>%</mo></math> of carbon in the organic compound is (Nearest integer)
question: On complete combustion, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>492</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an organic compound gave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>792</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>%</mo></math> of carbon in the organic compound is (Nearest integer)
solution:
44 grams of CO2 contain 12 grams of carbon.

Hence, one gram of carbon dioxide contain 1244 gram of carbon.

0.792 gram of carbon dioxide contain 1244 × 0.792 gram of carbon

Percentage of carbon = Mass of carbonMass of organic compound × 100

= 1244 × 0.7920.492 ×100

= 43.90%

So, answer is 44.

subject: Chemistry
q: The hydrocarbon $(X)$ with molar mass $80 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $90 \%$ carbon has $\ldots\ldots$ degree of unsaturation.
question: The hydrocarbon $(X)$ with molar mass $80 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $90 \%$ carbon has $\ldots\ldots$ degree of unsaturation.
solution: $\therefore$ Empirical formula is $\mathrm{C}_3 \mathrm{H}_4$ Molecular mass $=80 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. Molecular formula $=\left(\mathrm{C}_3 \mathrm{H}_4\right)_{\mathrm{n}}$ $\mathrm{n}=\frac{\text { Molecular mass }}{\mathrm{EF} \text { mass }}=\frac{80}{40}=2$ $\therefore$ Molecular formula is $\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_8$ Degree of unsaturation $=\frac{2 \times 6+2-8}{2}=3$

subject: Chemistry
q:
What volume of hydrogen gas at STP would be liberated by action of $50\text{ mL}$ of $H_2SO_4$ of $50\%$ purity (density $= 1.3\text{ g mL}^{-1}$) on $20\text{ g}$ of zinc ?
Given : Molar mass of H, O, S, Zn are $1, 16, 32, 65\text{ g mol}^{-1}$ respectively.
question:
What volume of hydrogen gas at STP would be liberated by action of $50\text{ mL}$ of $H_2SO_4$ of $50\%$ purity (density $= 1.3\text{ g mL}^{-1}$) on $20\text{ g}$ of zinc ?
Given : Molar mass of H, O, S, Zn are $1, 16, 32, 65\text{ g mol}^{-1}$ respectively.
solution:
Mass of $H_2SO_4$ solution $= 50 \times 1.3 = 65\text{ g}$

Mass of pure $H_2SO_4 = 65 \times \dfrac{50}{100} = 32.5\text{ g}$

Moles of $H_2SO_4 = \dfrac{32.5}{98} \approx 0.3316\text{ mol}$

Moles of $Zn = \dfrac{20}{65} \approx 0.3077\text{ mol}$

The balanced chemical equation is:
$Zn + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow ZnSO_4 + H_2$

Since the moles of $Zn$ are less than the moles of $H_2SO_4$, $Zn$ is the limiting reagent.

Moles of $H_2$ produced $=$ Moles of $Zn = \dfrac{20}{65}\text{ mol}$

Volume of $H_2$ gas at STP $= \dfrac{20}{65} \times 22.4\text{ L} = 6.892\text{ L}$

Answer: $6.892\text{ L}$

subject: Chemistry
q: The density of ' \(x\) ' \(\mathrm{M}\) solution (' \(X\) ' molar) of \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) is \(1.12 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}\), while in molality, the concentration of the solution is \(3 \mathrm{~m}(3 \mathrm{molal})\). Then \(x\) is (Given : Molar mass of \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) is \(40 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}\) )
question: The density of ' \(x\) ' \(\mathrm{M}\) solution (' \(X\) ' molar) of \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) is \(1.12 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}\), while in molality, the concentration of the solution is \(3 \mathrm{~m}(3 \mathrm{molal})\). Then \(x\) is (Given : Molar mass of \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) is \(40 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}\) )
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Molality }=\frac{1000 \times \mathrm{M}}{1000 \times \mathrm{d}-\mathrm{M} \times(\mathrm{Mw})_{\text {solute }}} \\ & 3=\frac{1000 \times \mathrm{x}}{1000 \times 1.12-(\mathrm{x} \times 40)} \\ & \mathrm{x}=3\end{aligned}$

q:
The minimum amount of O2g consumed per gram of reactant is for the reaction:

(Given atomic mass: Fe=56, O=16, Mg=24, P=31, C=12,H=1 )
question:
The minimum amount of O2g consumed per gram of reactant is for the reaction:

(Given atomic mass: Fe=56, O=16, Mg=24, P=31, C=12,H=1 )
options: <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
(i) 5 mole O2 required = 1 mole of P4 =124 gm P4

for per gram, O2  required = 5124 mole 

(ii) 1 mole O2 required = 2 mole Mg = 2×24 gm

for per gram, O2  required =148 mole

(iii) 3 mole O2 required = 2 mole Fe = 4×56 gm

for per gram Fe, O2 required =3112×2 mole

(iv) 5 mole O2 required - =1 mole C3H8 =44 gm C3H8

per gram C3H8, O2 required =544 mole

Therefore moles of PbSO4 formed

3 moles are present in 1 litre solution
$\text {molality }=\frac{3 \times 1000}{1.25 \times 1000-[3 \times 58.5]}=2.79 \mathrm{~m}$

Percentage of carbon=1244XxWX100                                       =1244X330120X100                                        =75%Percentage of Hydrogen =218XyWX100                                       =218X270120X100                                        =25%

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following have same number of significant figures?
(A) 0.00253
(B) 1.0003
(C) 15.0
(D)163
Choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Which of the following have same number of significant figures?
(A) 0.00253
(B) 1.0003
(C) 15.0
(D)163
Choose the correct answer from the options given below
solution:
All nonzero digits are significant. All zeros that are found between nonzero digits are significant. Leading zeros (to the left of the first nonzero digit) are not significant. Trailing zeros for a whole number that ends with a decimal point are significant.The number of significant figures of the given numbers are as follows:
Numbers                   No. of significant figures
(A) 0.00253                 3  
(B) 1.0003                   5
(C) 15.0                        3
(D) 163                         3
∴ (A), (C) and (D) have the same number of significant figures.

question: Complete combustion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an oxygen containing compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> gave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>64</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>08</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The percentage of oxygen in the organic compound is :
solution:
nCO2=2.6444=0.06

nc=0.06

weight of carbon =0.06×12=0.72 gm

nH2O=1.0818=0.06

nH=0.06×2=0.12

 weight of H=0.12 gm

= 1.8 - (0.72 + 0.12)

= 0.96 gram

 % weight of oxygen =0.961.8×100

The percentage of oxygen in the organic compound = 53.3%.

subject: Chemistry
q: Number of moles of methane required to produce <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>22</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi>CO</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow></msub></math> after combustion is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> moles. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is
question: Number of moles of methane required to produce <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>22</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi>CO</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow></msub></math> after combustion is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> moles. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is
solution:
CH4+2O2→CO2+2H2O

Gram molecular mass of CO2=12+2(16)=44 g/mol

From the reaction, it is clear that 1 mole of methane on complete combustion produces 44 g (1 mole) of carbon dioxide.

Therefore moles of CH4 required to produce 22 g of CO2 are:

=144×22=0.5 mol =50×10-2 mol

Hence, m=50

subject: Chemistry
q: At <math><mn>300</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> and <math><mn>1</mn></math> atmospheric pressure, <math><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of a hydrocarbon required <math><mn>55</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> for complete combustion, and <math><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is formed. The formula of the hydrocarbon is:
question: At <math><mn>300</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> and <math><mn>1</mn></math> atmospheric pressure, <math><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of a hydrocarbon required <math><mn>55</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> for complete combustion, and <math><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is formed. The formula of the hydrocarbon is:
solution:
Let hydrocarbon is CxHy

10x=40 ; x=4 and 10x+y4=55
x+y4=5510
x+y4=5.5
Put the value of x
4+y4=5.5
y4=5.5-4
y4=1.5 then y=6
Hence hydrocarbon is C4H6

q: The formula of a gaseous hydrocarbon which requires 6 times of its own volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> for complete oxidation and produces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times its own volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> is ________.
question: The formula of a gaseous hydrocarbon which requires 6 times of its own volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> for complete oxidation and produces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times its own volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> is ________.
solution:
Combustion reaction.

CxHy(g)+x+y4O2g→xCO2g+y2H2Oℓ

V               6 V             --                -                        Vx=4V

⇒x=4

Since: I Vo2=6×VCxHy

⇒Vx+y4=6 V

⇒x+y4=6⇒4+y4=6

⇒y=8

question: What quantity (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mL</mi></math>) of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>%</mo></math> acid solution of a mono-protic strong acid must be mixed with a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>%</mo></math> solution of the same acid to produce <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>800</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>29</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>875</mn><mo>%</mo></math> acid solution?

V×45100+800-V20100=800×29.875100

9V20+160-V5=239

5V20=79⇒  V=316

subject: Chemistry
q: The density of a solution prepared by dissolving $120 \mathrm{~g}$ of urea (mol. Mass $=60 \mathrm{u}$ ) in $1000 \mathrm{~g}$ of water is $1.15 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$. The molarity of this solution is :
question: The density of a solution prepared by dissolving $120 \mathrm{~g}$ of urea (mol. Mass $=60 \mathrm{u}$ ) in $1000 \mathrm{~g}$ of water is $1.15 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$. The molarity of this solution is :
solution:
Total weight of solution $=1000+120=1120 \mathrm{~g}$
$\text { Molarity }=\frac{120}{60} \times \frac{1000}{1120 / 1.15}=2.05 \mathrm{~M}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
How many grams of residue is obtained by heating $2.76$ g of silver carbonate?
(Given: Molar mass of C, O and Ag are $12$, $16$ and $108$ g mol$^{-1}$ respectively)
question:
How many grams of residue is obtained by heating $2.76$ g of silver carbonate?
(Given: Molar mass of C, O and Ag are $12$, $16$ and $108$ g mol$^{-1}$ respectively)
solution:
Molar mass of $Ag_2CO_3$ = $2 \times 108 + 12 + 3 \times 16 = 276$ g mol$^{-1}$

Number of moles of $Ag_2CO_3$ = $\dfrac{2.76}{276} = 0.01$ mol

The decomposition reaction of silver carbonate on heating is:

$Ag_2CO_3(s) \rightarrow 2Ag(s) + CO_2(g) + \dfrac{1}{2}O_2(g)$

From the stoichiometry of the reaction, $1$ mole of $Ag_2CO_3$ gives $2$ moles of Ag as residue.

Number of moles of Ag formed = $2 \times 0.01 = 0.02$ mol

Mass of Ag residue = $0.02 \times 108 = 2.16$ g

Answer: $2.16$ g

1→34

x=110×63×4101×3=91.5gm

subject: Chemistry
q:
Excess of NaOH (aq) was added to 100 mL of FeCl3 aq resulting into 2.14 g of FeOH3. The molarity of FeCl3aq is:

(Given the molar mass of Fe=56 g mol-1 and molar mass of Cl=35.5 g mol-1)
question:
Excess of NaOH (aq) was added to 100 mL of FeCl3 aq resulting into 2.14 g of FeOH3. The molarity of FeCl3aq is:

(Given the molar mass of Fe=56 g mol-1 and molar mass of Cl=35.5 g mol-1)
solution:
Moles of FeOH3 =2.14107=2×10-2mol

Moles FeCl3=100 ml × 10−3 × M

According to stoichiometry, mole of FeCl3=Mole of Fe(OH)3

100 × 10−3 ×M=2 × 10−2

M=2×10-2100×1000=0.2 M

subject: Chemistry
q: How many litres of water must be added to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> litre of aqueous solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCl</mi></math> with a pH of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> to create an aqueous solution with pH of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math>?
question: How many litres of water must be added to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> litre of aqueous solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCl</mi></math> with a pH of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> to create an aqueous solution with pH of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math>?
solution:
pH=1=-log [H+]

[H+]=10-1 M

Final pH=2; [H+]=10-2 M

M1V1=M2V2

(0.1 M× 1 Litre)=(0.01) V2

V2=10 L

Final volume = 10 L

Water added =10-1=9 L.

subject: Chemistry
q: The ratio of masses of oxygen and nitrogen in a particular gaseous mixture is 1 : 4. The ratio of number of their molecules is:
question: The ratio of masses of oxygen and nitrogen in a particular gaseous mixture is 1 : 4. The ratio of number of their molecules is:
solution:
Let the mass of oxygen be x gm
∴   Mass of nitrogen = 4x gm
Number of molecules = 6.023 x 1023 x number of moles
∴   Ratio = 6.023 × 1 0 2 3 × no. of moles of oxygen 6.023 × 1 0 2 3 × no. of moles of nitrogen
              = m O 2 M O 2 m N 2 M N 2 = x 3 2 4 x 2 8
              = 7 3 2 = 7 : 32
